skill.name: skill-creator
skill.description: Guides agents through creating, updating, scaffolding, and validating skills with clear metadata, reusable resources, and practical design patterns.
skill.summary: Create and validate agent skills with scaffolding, structure guidance, and reusable resource patterns.
skill.author: langchain-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most were false positives from Markdown backticks, documentation examples, and validation terminology. The skill does include executable Python scripts that create directories and files at user-selected paths, and the initializer does not enforce its documented skill-name restrictions before writing.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Filesystem Writes During Skill Initialization
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The initializer resolves a user-supplied output path, appends the supplied skill name, creates directories, writes SKILL.md and example resource files, and marks an example script executable. This is expected scaffolding behavior, but it can write outside an intended workspace if invoked with unsafe arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly creates directories and writes files using command-line arguments. The behavior is legitimate for a scaffolder, but the filesystem impact is real.

medium_findings.title: Documented Skill Name Rules Are Not Enforced By Initializer
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The initializer prints naming requirements but does not validate skill_name before using it in a filesystem path. This allows malformed names, including path-like values, to influence the created directory location.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The validator enforces naming rules elsewhere, but the initializer accepts sys.argv[1] without equivalent checks. The risk depends on how users invoke the script.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Hits Are Markdown And String False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported external command detections are backticks in Markdown examples or normal Python strings. I did not find evidence of shell execution, subprocess usage, eval, exec, or network-based command retrieval in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples or template text. The Python files contain no shell invocation API at the cited locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reviewed files do not implement hashing, encryption, signature checks, or cryptographic key handling. The static detections appear to match ordinary words in documentation and validation messages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operation at the cited locations. The signals are caused by natural-language content and variable names.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory References Are Documented User Paths
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: References to ~/.deepagents describe the expected Deepagents skills directory and examples for user-provided paths. They are not stealthy hidden-file access or persistence mechanisms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden path is presented as a documented install location for the CLI. No code automatically scans or modifies that path unless a user supplies it.

low_findings.title: Certificate Or Key File Static Hit Is A Frontmatter Key Check
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The quick validator computes unexpected YAML frontmatter keys and reports allowed keys. This is schema validation, not certificate, private key, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code compares dictionary keys against an allowed property set. No certificate file, private key file, or secret material is read.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Hits Are Local Validation Checks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported reconnaissance lines validate frontmatter formatting, file presence, and string rules for a local skill directory. I did not find evidence of host fingerprinting or data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The checks are limited to the supplied skill path and documentation guidance. No OS inventory, network enumeration, or secret discovery behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Construction From Unsanitized CLI Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The initializer combines a user-provided base path and skill name before creating directories and files. Add skill-name validation before path construction to reduce accidental writes outside the intended skills folder.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction is explicit and unsanitized. The risk is bounded because the script is a local user-invoked scaffolder.

dangerous_patterns.title: Executable File Creation In Generated Skill Resources
dangerous_patterns.description: The initializer creates an example Python script and marks it executable. The current content is benign, but generated executable resources increase review requirements for marketplace publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The chmod call is present and affects a generated script. The generated script only prints a placeholder message, so this is a moderate operational risk, not malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable guidance for building skills that agents can use reliably. This skill provides structure, scaffolding, validation, and design patterns for creating practical skills.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required SKILL.md structure and frontmatter fields.
Guides users through planning reusable scripts, references, and assets.
Provides an initializer script that creates a starter skill directory.
Provides a quick validator for frontmatter and naming checks.
Describes progressive disclosure patterns for large skill content.
Gives examples for workflow, task, reference, and capability based skills.
limitations: The initializer writes files to user-supplied paths and needs careful invocation.
The validator performs basic checks only and is not a full security scanner.
Generated templates contain placeholders that users must replace before publishing.
The skill focuses on Deepagents style skills and may need adaptation for other runtimes.

use_cases.title: Scaffold a new agent skill
use_cases.description: Create a starter folder with SKILL.md, scripts, references, and assets placeholders.

use_cases.title: Improve an existing skill
use_cases.description: Refine metadata, move long details into references, and align resources with real workflows.

use_cases.title: Validate marketplace readiness
use_cases.description: Check basic frontmatter rules, naming conventions, and description quality before review.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user creating a first skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a skill for [task]. Ask only the most important questions first, then scaffold the skill structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan reusable resources
prompt_templates.scenario: User knows the workflow but needs a skill design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a skill for [workflow]. Identify which instructions belong in SKILL.md and which resources should become scripts, references, or assets.

prompt_templates.title: Update an existing skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this existing skill for clarity, progressive disclosure, and unnecessary files. Recommend focused edits and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for publication
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this skill for metadata quality, trigger accuracy, resource organization, validation errors, and security review concerns.

output_examples.input: Create a skill for rotating and splitting PDF files.
output: A proposed skill name and frontmatter description.
A compact SKILL.md outline with task based sections.
A recommendation to place deterministic PDF operations in scripts.
A list of unclear trigger phrases to revise.
A plan to move schema details into reference files.
Validation steps for name, description, and frontmatter fields.

output_examples.input: Check whether this skill is ready for review.
output: A readiness summary covering structure, metadata, and bundled resources.
Specific fixes for placeholders, long descriptions, or unsupported fields.

best_practices: Keep SKILL.md concise and move detailed material into clearly referenced files.
Test bundled scripts with representative inputs before publishing the skill.
Write frontmatter descriptions that state both capability and exact trigger contexts.
anti_patterns: Do not add broad documentation files that do not support the agent workflow.
Do not leave generated TODO placeholders in published skill content.
Do not store secrets, private keys, or environment specific credentials in skill assets.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create a skill folder with SKILL.md and optional scripts, references, and assets.

faq.question: Does it validate every security issue?
faq.answer: No. The included validator checks basic structure and metadata only.

faq.question: Can it update existing skills?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides guidance for refining metadata, content structure, and bundled resources.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible skill workflows.

faq.question: Why are scripts included?
faq.answer: Scripts provide repeatable behavior for tasks that need deterministic execution or repeated automation.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should review filesystem behavior, remove placeholders, test scripts, and confirm metadata accuracy.
